Visceral disseminated VZV infection is a rare but serious complication with a high mortality rate in immunosuppressed patients such as hematological diseases or organ transplant recipients [2, 4, 6, 7, 13]. Ishikawa et al. [14] reported that visceral disseminated VZV infection occurred in 3,051 (10.5%) of 29,054 hospitalized patients with herpes zoster, and over 75 years of age, liver cirrhosis, heart failure and previous cerebrovascular events were poorer prognostic factors in-hospital mortality, although there are no matching factors in our case. In patients with SLE, visceral disseminated VZV infection is rare, although Habuka et al. [8] and Vassia et al. [9] recently reported the fatal cases of visceral disseminated VZV infection in patients with SLE undergoing immunosuppressive therapy consisting of PSL and MMF.

Visceral disseminated VZV infection is an uncommon yet severe complication that presents a high mortality rate among individuals with weakened immune systems, such as those with hematological diseases or organ transplants [2, 4, 6, 7, 13]. According to Ishikawa et al. [14], around 10.5% (3,051 out of 29,054) of hospitalized patients with herpes zoster experienced visceral disseminated VZV infection. The study identified age over 75, liver cirrhosis, heart failure, and prior cerebrovascular events as negative factors associated with in-hospital mortality. However, these factors do not align with our particular case. Although rare in patients with SLE, there have been recent reports by Habuka et al. [8] and Vassia et al. [9] documenting fatal cases of visceral disseminated VZV infection in SLE patients undergoing immunosuppressive therapy involving PSL and MMF.

The symptoms, fever, breathing difficulty, abdominal pain and skin blisters, and clinical course of our presented case were typical as disseminated VZV infection. In most cases of visceral disseminated VZV infection, gastrointestinal symptom including abdominal pain, intestinal obstruction, vomiting and diarrhea preceded skin lesions by several days (range 4–10 days) [2]. Of gastrointestinal symptoms, the incidence of abdominal pain was 100%, although there was no specific finding in abdominal ultrasound and CT scan [2, 15,16,17]. The management of abdominal pain is generally difficult and requires opioid analgesics [18, 19]. But in our case, her abdominal pain resolved after the administration of antiviral therapy for 4 days. The severe abdominal pain may be resulted from proliferation or inflammation by VZV in the celiac and mesenteric ganglia [19, 20], although the precise mechanism remains unclear. As seen in our patient, other such patients also presented with moderately or profoundly elevated levels of transaminase and coagulation abnormalities [15, 18].
Chakravarty et al. [3] also showed that patients with SLE had an increased susceptibility for VZV compared to patients with other musculoskeletal diseases, and the use of immunosuppressive agents were an independent risk factor for the VZV infection in patients with SLE. Furthermore, Rondaan et al. [27] reported that both cellular and humoral immunity to VZV weakened in patients with SLE.

The clinical presentation of our case, including symptoms like fever, difficulty breathing, abdominal pain, and skin blisters, aligns with the typical manifestations of disseminated VZV infection. In most instances of visceral disseminated VZV infection, gastrointestinal symptoms such as abdominal pain, intestinal obstruction, vomiting, and diarrhea tend to appear a few days (ranging from 4 to 10 days) before the onset of skin lesions [2]. Abdominal pain, in particular, is reported in all cases, despite no specific ultrasound or CT scan findings [2, 15, 16, 17]. Usually, managing abdominal pain is challenging and necessitates the use of opioid analgesics [18, 19]. However, in our case, the patient's abdominal pain subsided after four days of antiviral therapy. The severity of abdominal pain may be attributed to VZV proliferation or inflammation in the celiac and mesenteric ganglia [19, 20], although the exact mechanism remains unclear. Coinciding with our patient's condition, other similar cases also exhibited significantly increased transaminase levels and coagulation abnormalities [15, 18].

Chakravarty et al. [3] also found that individuals with SLE were more susceptible to VZV infection compared to those with other musculoskeletal disorders, with the use of immunosuppressive agents being an independent risk factor for VZV infection among SLE patients. Additionally, Rondaan et al. [27] reported that both cellular and humoral immunity to VZV was weakened in SLE patients.

Of the various treatment options for disseminated VZV infection, antiviral therapy plays a critical role in managing this condition. Early initiation of antiviral treatment is crucial to prevent further complications and improve patient outcomes. Intravenous administration of acyclovir is commonly recommended as the first-line therapy for visceral disseminated VZV infection [2, 21]. The optimal duration of antiviral treatment may differ depending on the severity of the infection, the patient's immune status, and the presence of associated complications. In severe cases, prolonged treatment courses may be necessary to achieve complete resolution of symptoms and prevent relapses. Concurrent management of pain, supportive care, and close monitoring of potential organ-specific complications are essential components of the comprehensive management of disseminated VZV infection [2, 22]. Tailoring the treatment approach to each patient's unique clinical situation is crucial to optimize outcomes and minimize the risks associated with this serious infection [23].

The prognosis of visceral disseminated VZV infection often depends on various factors such as the underlying immune status of the patient and the presence of comorbidities. In immunosuppressed individuals, such as those with hematological diseases or organ transplant recipients, the mortality rate associated with this infection is notably high [2, 4, 6, 7, 13]. It has been observed that advanced age, liver cirrhosis, heart failure, and previous cerebrovascular events are negative prognostic factors for in-hospital mortality [14]. However, in our specific case, these factors did not align. While visceral disseminated VZV infection is rare in patients with SLE, recent reports have highlighted fatal cases in SLE patients who were undergoing immunosuppressive therapy [8, 9].

Currently, there is a lack of definite recommendations for the treatment of SLE with severe varicella, but varicella infection following organ or stem cell transplantation has already been reported. With corresponding therapeutic experience as the reference, the following aspects should be taken into account for treating severe varicella in critically ill patients with rapidly progressive disease: (1) Given that maintaining respiratory and circulatory stability is the premise for successful rescue, life support must be a priority; (2) early addition of antiviral drugs contributes to reducing tissue injury and diminishing or even preventing the destruction of affected ganglion cells[16]. In general, antiviral therapy should be continued until all rashes have dried and organ symptoms have resolved; (3) in the presence of severe complications, intravenous injection of human immunoglobulin can be used to control virus invasion and suppress toxaemia-related antibodies, exerting a synergistic effect with antiviral drugs in the clinical treatment of varicella[17]; (4) glucocorticoids repress synthesis of interferon in the reticuloendothelial system, thus lowering the number of WBCs involved in phagocytosis and facilitating proliferation and spread of the virus in the body. Hence, glucocorticoid therapy is not recommended for mild VZV infection but for patients with severe pneumonia complications. Administration of methylprednisolone sodium succinate can reduce inflammatory reactions, suppress release of inflammatory mediators and cytokines and diminish exudation, thus achieving favourable curative effects[18]; (5) complications should be actively prevented and treated; and (6) the varicella-zoster vaccine should be considered for patients with autoimmune diseases[19].

There are currently no established treatment guidelines specifically for the management of severe varicella in individuals with SLE, although cases of varicella infection following organ or stem cell transplantation have been documented. Based on therapeutic experiences in these cases, certain considerations should be taken into account when treating critically ill patients with rapidly progressive disease. Firstly, ensuring respiratory and circulatory stability is crucial for successful rescue, with life support measures prioritized. Secondly, early initiation of antiviral drugs is beneficial in reducing tissue damage and preventing the destruction of affected ganglion cells. Typically, antiviral therapy is continued until all rashes have dried and organ symptoms have resolved. In cases with severe complications, intravenous human immunoglobulin may be utilized to control virus invasion and suppress toxaemia-related antibodies, complementing the effect of antiviral drugs. Although glucocorticoids can impede interferon synthesis in the reticuloendothelial system, lowering the number of white blood cells involved in phagocytosis and potentially facilitating virus proliferation, their use is recommended primarily for patients with severe pneumonia complications. Administration of methylprednisolone sodium succinate can help alleviate inflammatory reactions, restrict the release of inflammatory mediators and cytokines, and reduce exudation. Active prevention and treatment of complications should also be implemented, and consideration should be given to the potential use of the varicella-zoster vaccine in autoimmune disease patients.

Currently, there is a lack of established treatment guidelines specifically for managing severe varicella in patients with SLE. However, reports have documented cases of varicella infection following organ or stem cell transplantation. Drawing from therapeutic experiences in these cases, certain aspects should be considered when dealing with critically ill patients who have rapidly progressive disease due to severe varicella. Firstly, maintaining respiratory and circulatory stability is of utmost importance in ensuring successful rescue, with life support measures given top priority. Secondly, early administration of antiviral drugs can help minimize tissue injury and potentially prevent the destruction of affected ganglion cells. Typically, antiviral therapy is continued until all rashes have dried and organ symptoms have resolved. In cases with severe complications, intravenous injection of human immunoglobulin may be used to control viral invasion and suppress toxemia-related antibodies, complementing the effect of antiviral drugs in clinical varicella treatment. While mild cases of VZV infection do not warrant glucocorticoid therapy, patients with severe pneumonia complications may benefit from it. Administration of methylprednisolone sodium succinate can help reduce inflammatory reactions, inhibit the release of inflammatory mediators and cytokines, and decrease exudation, thus leading to favorable therapeutic outcomes. Actively preventing and treating complications is crucial, and considering the varicella-zoster vaccine may be an option for patients with autoimmune diseases.

There is currently no standardized treatment protocol for addressing severe varicella in patients with SLE. However, previous instances of varicella infection post organ or stem cell transplantation have been documented. Based on these therapeutic experiences, certain considerations should be taken into account when managing critically ill patients with rapidly progressing disease. Firstly, ensuring the stability of respiratory and circulatory functions is crucial for successful intervention, with priority given to life support measures. Secondly, early initiation of antiviral medications can help mitigate tissue damage and prevent the destruction of ganglion cells affected by the infection. Generally, antiviral therapy should be continued until all rashes have subsided and organ-related symptoms have resolved. In cases with severe complications, intravenous human immunoglobulin treatment may be employed to control the invasion of the virus and suppress antibodies linked to its toxic effects, working synergistically with antiviral drugs. Glucocorticoids, known to inhibit interferon synthesis in the reticuloendothelial system, may be utilized for patients with severe pneumonia complications rather than mild VZV infections. The administration of methylprednisolone sodium succinate can help attenuate inflammatory reactions, suppress the release of mediators and cytokines, and reduce fluid accumulation, ultimately yielding favorable treatment outcomes. Active prevention and management of complications are crucial, and consideration should be given to potential varicella-zoster vaccination for patients with autoimmune conditions.

At present, there are no specific treatment recommendations for managing severe varicella in individuals with SLE. However, cases of varicella infection following organ or stem cell transplantations have been previously reported. Drawing from therapeutic experiences in those cases, several factors should be considered when addressing severe varicella in critically ill patients with rapidly progressive disease. First and foremost, ensuring respiratory and circulatory stability is crucial for successful patient outcomes, with a primary focus on providing life support. Additionally, early administration of antiviral drugs can effectively reduce tissue damage and prevent the destruction of ganglion cells affected by the virus. Antiviral therapy is typically continued until all rashes have healed and organ-related symptoms have subsided. In cases involving severe complications, intravenous human immunoglobulin can be administered to control viral invasion and neutralize toxemia-related antibodies, working alongside antiviral medications for a synergistic therapeutic effect. While glucocorticoids can suppress interferon synthesis in the reticuloendothelial system, reducing the number of white blood cells involved in phagocytosis and potentially aiding virus proliferation, their use is predominantly recommended for patients with severe pneumonia complications. The administration of methylprednisolone sodium succinate can help alleviate inflammatory reactions, limit the release of inflammatory mediators and cytokines, and decrease exudation, leading to favorable treatment outcomes. Active prevention and treatment of complications should be prioritized, and the consideration of varicella-zoster vaccination may be beneficial for patients with autoimmune diseases.

Currently, there is no established treatment protocol specifically tailored for severe varicella in patients diagnosed with SLE. However, cases of varicella infection following organ or stem cell transplantation have been reported. Drawing from therapeutic experiences in these instances, there are several key considerations to keep in mind when managing critically ill patients with rapidly progressive varicella. Firstly, ensuring the stability of respiratory and circulatory functions is paramount for successful intervention, with a primary focus on providing life support measures. Secondly, the early initiation of antiviral medications can be beneficial in reducing tissue damage and preventing the destruction of affected ganglion cells. Generally, antiviral therapy is continued until all rashes have resolved and organ-specific symptoms have improved. In cases with severe complications, intravenous administration of human immunoglobulin may be considered to control viral invasion and suppress the presence of toxemia-associated antibodies, thereby working synergistically with antiviral drugs. While glucocorticoids have the potential to inhibit interferon synthesis in the reticuloendothelial system, which can lead to an increase in the number of white blood cells involved in phagocytosis and facilitate the spread of the virus, their use is typically reserved for patients with severe pneumonia complications rather than mild varicella infections. A treatment regimen involving methylprednisolone sodium succinate may help in reducing inflammatory reactions, limiting the release of inflammatory mediators and cytokines, and alleviating excess fluid accumulation, ultimately promoting positive treatment outcomes. Vigilance in preventing and treating complications is crucial, and the potential of varicella-zoster vaccination should be considered for patients with autoimmune diseases.

The timing of reinitiating immunosuppressive agents for controlling SLE remains obscure, and no definite therapeutic evidence has been reported. Previous literature on VZV infection after renal transplantation has suggested that if immunosuppressive agents cannot be discontinued, such agents should be replaced with cyclosporine, azathioprine and prednisone[22]; mycophenolate mofetil may increase the incidence rate of severe varicella[23]. If high-dose steroids such as mycophenolate mofetil and cyclophosphamide are required because of disease conditions, herpes zoster virus reactivation should be strongly suspected. Specifically, elevated VZV-IgM and VZV-DNA without clinical symptoms indicates subclinical reactivation, and the appearance of herpes zoster or varicella suggests clinical reactivation. In addition, VZV-IgG levels persist during infection[24,25] and are thus of no value for determining the presence or absence of varicella infection. In this case, the patient’s family members refused to undergo VZV-DNA assessment during the recovery period; however, after communication, the patient was given wysolone (20 mg once daily) to treat SLE. Regular follow-up showed no varicella
